---
# =====================================================================
#  Research highlight — one- to two-page PDF (LaTeX/PDF via Quarto)
#  Canonical layout: see style-guides/research-highlight/README.md
#  Render:  quarto render research-highlights-template.qmd
#  Design system: _style.tex (colours, fonts, boxes, macros)
#  Figure: build with a script in code/ and save to figures/
#
#  LENGTH — use the space the paper warrants; pick ONE budget, stay close:
#    One page  | Background ~120 | Found ~150 + 1 fig | 3 Takeaways ~50 ea
#    Two pages | Background ~180 | Found ~300 + 1-2 figs | 3-4 Takeaways ~60 ea
#  Don't shrink the layout to fit, and don't pad to fill a second page.
# =====================================================================
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    documentclass: article
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - top=0.4in
      - bottom=0.45in
      - left=0.65in
      - right=0.65in
      - includehead
      - headheight=52pt
      - headsep=7pt
      - includefoot
      - footskip=24pt
    include-in-header:
      - file: ../../../formats/research-highlight/_style.tex
      - text: |
          % --- Running masthead + footer (logo, label, rule, page number) ---
          \usepackage{fancyhdr}
          \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0pt}
          \renewcommand{\footrulewidth}{0pt}
          \fancypagestyle{brief}{%
            \fancyhf{}%
            \fancyhead[L]{%
              \begin{minipage}[c]{0.62\textwidth}\raggedright\color{ink}\includegraphics[height=0.45in]{../../../formats/logos/eil-logo-maroon.png}\end{minipage}\hfill%
              \begin{minipage}[c]{0.36\textwidth}\raggedleft\fontsize{8.25pt}{10pt}\selectfont\bfseries\color{accentred}\textls[200]{\MakeUppercase{Research Highlight}}\end{minipage}%
              \par\vspace{2pt}%
              \noindent\textcolor{ink}{\rule{\textwidth}{2.2pt}}%
            }%
            \fancyfoot[C]{%
              \color{warmrule}\rule{\textwidth}{0.7pt}\par\vspace{3pt}%
              \hbox to\textwidth{%
                \color{faint}\fontsize{7.4pt}{9pt}\selectfont
                For full paper:\hspace{0.4em}\href{https://doi.org/[DOI]}{\color{faint}\underline{\textls[40]{doi.org/[DOI]}}}%
                \hfill
                Page \thepage%
              }%
            }%
          }
          \pagestyle{brief}
          % Takeaways subheads in the brand maroon (logo / Research Highlight)
          \renewcommand{\subhead}[1]{%
            {\color{accentred}\bfseries\fontsize{8.25pt}{10pt}\selectfont
            \textls[50]{\MakeUppercase{#1}}\par}\vspace{2pt}}
    keep-tex: true
---

```{=latex}
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% TITLE + CITATION  (masthead repeats in the page header)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\brieftitle{[Title — state the finding as a claim, not a topic]}
\briefcitation{Lessons from ``[Exact Paper Title]'' by [Authors] in \textit{[Journal]}, [Year].}

\vspace{-2pt}

% override body font size for this document
\fontsize{10.5pt}{13.5pt}\selectfont

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BOTTOM LINE  (~40–50 words)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{bottomline}
{\fontsize{11pt}{15pt}\selectfont
\textbf{[The core finding in one bold sentence.]} [One or two sentences expanding on the finding and why it matters.]
}
\end{bottomline}

\vspace{6pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BACKGROUND  (~120 words, 2 paragraphs)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Background}
\noindent [Set up the policy problem. Define any key term on first use in \textbf{bold}, then explain it plainly. Spell out agencies on first mention. End on the open question the study tests.]

\noindent [Describe the setting and data in plain language — who was studied, what records the researchers had, and over what period.]

\vspace{6pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% WHAT THE STUDY FOUND  (~150 words + figure)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{What the study found}
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% FIGURE — one figure, centered. Build via a script in code/ -> figures/
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{center}
\begin{minipage}{0.82\linewidth}
\figtitle{[Sentence-style figure title — what the chart shows]}{Source: [Author(s)] ([Year]).}
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{../data-viz/figures/fig1-college-attendance.png}
\par\vspace{3pt}
{\color{faint}\fontsize{7.1pt}{9.5pt}\selectfont \textit{Note:} Each point compares one parent's birth cohort with the 1971 cohort — whether their children were more or less likely to attend college. The line is flat before 1971 and climbs after the 1970 Clean Air Act cleared the air: by the 1975 cohort, children of parents who grew up with cleaner air were markedly more likely to attend college. The shaded band shows the range of uncertainty, pinched to zero at the 1971 comparison year.\par}
\end{minipage}
\end{center}

\vspace{6pt}

\noindent [Explain why the research design is credible — narrate the comparison, don't name the method. What would the result look like if the policy worked?]

\noindent [State the result, pointing the reader to the figure. End with the headline finding in \textbf{bold}.]

\vspace{8pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% TAKEAWAYS  (3 points, ~50 words each)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Takeaways}
\subhead{[Subhead 1]}
\noindent [First takeaway. Bold the one sentence someone skimming should catch.]

\vspace{5pt}
\subhead{[Subhead 2]}
\noindent [Second takeaway.]

\vspace{5pt}
\subhead{[Subhead 3]}
\noindent [Third takeaway. Acknowledge any caveats or limitations without getting too technical, then restate the central point.]

\vspace{8pt}

\begin{infobox}
\textbf{For more information}, contact Jonathan Colmer (j.colmer@virginia.edu).\par
\vspace{2pt}
Colmer, Jonathan, and John Voorheis. Forthcoming. ``The Intergenerational Effects of Early-Life Pollution Exposure.'' \textit{Journal of Political Economy: Microeconomics}. \href{https://doi.org/10.1086/740104}{\color{faint}\underline{doi.org/10.1086/740104}}.\par
\end{infobox}
```
